My house was built on a foundation of wood. It works well normally because I put a whole lot of wood under it. 

But termites came and started to eat the wood. Slowly but surely, the wood got thinned out. My house still was fine. Everybody was still living inside comfortably. I ignored the termites since nobody was complaining and didn't add more wood back. The new narrative was a glut of wood anyway, don't worry.

Suddenly, a big storm came. My shaky wooden foundation crumbled. All hell broke loose. But my neighbour next door with cement foundation was fine. 

How I wish I fixed that termite issue when I had the time.

COT on #commodities covering the week to 14 July captured a broad improvement in risk appetite across the sector, with the Bloomberg Commodity Total Return Index (BCOM) rising 3.1%, led by a powerful rally in energy (excluding natural gas), alongside renewed strength in copper, soybean oil and wheat.

Energy positioning saw the biggest shift. The net long in Brent crude more than doubled to almost 170,000 contracts as an aggressive bout of short covering combined with fresh long buying following the renewed escalation of Middle East tensions. Together with additional buying in WTI, the combined speculative net long climbed to around 240,000 contracts, the highest in five weeks, although still well below the peaks seen earlier this year. In contrast, the 11% weekly slump in US natural gas encouraged fresh bearish positioning, with the net short widening to more than 50,000 contracts, the largest in 27 months.

Across metals, gold attracted modest net buying despite a weekly price decline, highlighting continued strategic interest from money managers. Silver sentiment softened, with the net long reduced by 14%, while copper buying resumed after several weeks of position trimming, lifting the net long by 3% as tightening visible inventories and renewed optimism over industrial demand improved sentiment.

The grain sector also experienced a notable shift in positioning. Buying was driven primarily by short covering as Middle East and renewed Black Sea tensions and weather concerns forced traders to reassess the outlook. Even after a 41% reduction in the net short, speculative funds remained meaningfully short CBOT wheat heading into the reporting week's close, leaving the market vulnerable to the sharp rally that unfolded in the days immediately afterwards as Black Sea export disruption fears intensified.

COT on #forex covering the week to 14 July showed speculative appetite for the US dollar beginning to ease after a sharp multi-week build-up drove the aggregate non-commercial long against eight IMM currency futures to a 10-year high above USD 40 billion. During the latest reporting week, that net long was trimmed by 4% to USD 39.3 billion, suggesting some early profit-taking rather than a decisive shift in sentiment.

The reduction was driven primarily by short covering in EUR, CHF and, most notably, GBP, and only partly offset by continued selling AUD and CAD. Despite the modest pullback, overall positioning remains heavily skewed in favour of the dollar, reflecting continued expectations that US interest rates will stay elevated relative to most other developed markets.

As the table shows, speculative traders remain net long the dollar against all but one of the currencies tracked. The largest bearish bets continue to be held in CAD, JPY and GBP, while the euro remains close to neutral. The persistence of sizeable short positions across most major currencies leaves the market vulnerable to further bouts of short covering should the dollar lose momentum on softer US data, a further easing in rate expectations or a deeper stock market correction leading to overseas sale of the greenback.

Just in: Upgrade to "overweight"! The latest comments from foreign investors are related to Chinese stocks.

Wall Street giants made a big statement. According to the latest news, Wall Street giant Citigroup has adjusted its asset allocation recommendations for emerging markets and raised its rating on China's stock market to "overweight." Citi strategists have given the MSCI China Index a target price of $92 at the end of 2026, with a potential upside of 31%.

At the same time, Citigroup downgraded the Korean market rating from "overweight" to "tactical neutral" on the grounds that the Korean market trading is turbulent and the KOSPI implied volatility has far exceeded similar global markets.

Morgan Stanley also lowered its pessimistic scenario target for South Korea's KOSPI index from 6,500 points to 6,000 points. Citi: Overweight Chinese assets Citi Research wrote in the latest "Emerging Market Equity Strategy Outlook for the Second Half of 2026" that it raised the rating of Chinese stocks from "neutral" to "overweight".

This year's rally led by a few artificial intelligence winners is expected to spread to a wider range of sectors, and the Chinese market is the preferred target for capital rotation. Analysts from the bank pointed out that a true “diffusion market” requires two conditions to be met at the same time: 1.

Evidence of cyclical recovery - macro data improves, and earnings upward revisions spread to a wider range of sectors and regions. 2. The rising momentum led by AI technology has come to a periodic pause - leaving room for catching up with the relative performance of other sectors.

Citigroup believes that, at present, the above two conditions are "partially met." Citi strategists including David Groman wrote in a report that there is room for scope expansion if the macro environment continues to be favorable, including easing geopolitical risks.

Citi pointed out in the report that China is a strong candidate for "spreading the market" - the macro environment of light positioning, falling oil prices and improving global growth is favorable to it, and valuations remain attractive.

Citi China strategist Pierre Lau pointed out that the Hang Seng Index is currently trading at 9.4 times the expected 2026 price-earnings ratio and 1.1 times the price-to-book ratio, both lower than the historical average (10.3 times the price-earnings ratio, 1.2 times the price-to-book ratio).

Citigroup has set a target price of 29,600 points for the Hang Seng Index at the end of 2026 and a target price of 30,500 points in mid-2027; the CSI 300 target prices are 5,600 points and 5,700 points respectively; the MSCI China Index target prices are US$92 (end 2026) and US$97 (mid-2027), respectively, with about 31% upside potential from the current level.

Citigroup maintains the MSCI emerging market year-end target price of 1,870 points, with about 12% upside from the current level, and introduces a mid-2027 target price of 2,050 points (about 20% upside) for the first time.

The target price is based on conservative EPS growth assumptions (about 40%-45%, lower than market consensus expectations) and a slight contraction in valuation multiples. Prior to this, Standard Chartered Bank also maintained an "overweight" rating on Chinese stocks in its report.

The core reason is that there is still a significant valuation discount compared to major global markets. Goldman Sachs also maintains its "overweight" position on A-shares.

Morgan Stanley said that recent global roadshow feedback shows that international investors' interest in Chinese stocks is heating up, and it is expected that there will be a trend of gradual reallocation of funds in the coming months.

UBS Securities China stock strategy analysts said that the profit growth rate of all A-shares this year is expected to increase from 3.9% last year to 11%. Citi also stated in the report that it will not completely withdraw its exposure to AI technology due to short-term fluctuations. The main reasons are as follows: 1.

The free cash flow (FCF) of Asian memory manufacturers is expected to rise significantly from 2026 to 2027, which is in sharp contrast to the FCF of ultra-large-scale cloud manufacturers in the United States approaching zero, indicating that the profit pool of the global technology sector is still expanding. 2.

Citi Korea’s local strategist believes that the memory shortage signal will be further strengthened in 2027, and the trend of memory customization and the growth of AI tokens will promote the continuation of the upward cycle. 3.

The fundamentals of the technology sector in emerging markets remain solid: the EPS growth rate of the IT sector far exceeds that of its global counterparts, profit increases continue, and valuations are attractive compared to similar companies.

Citigroup also said in the report that it downgraded the Korean stock market rating from "overweight" to "tactical neutral" due to the extreme fluctuations in Korean chip stocks in recent weeks. During the session on July 20, South Korea's KOSPI index once fell by more than 5%.

As of the close, the decline reached 4.46%; memory chip giants SK Hynix and Samsung Electronics fell by 4.23% and 4.31% respectively.

Citigroup said that almost all client discussions are currently focusing on the prospect that market performance may "expand" (that is, spread to more sectors) in the second half of 2026, but in the allocation of emerging market countries, the bank is still cautious about fully withdrawing from the technology sector.

At the same time, Morgan Stanley lowered its pessimistic scenario target for South Korea's KOSPI index from 6,500 points to 6,000 points, saying that the index's sharp rise earlier this year made many investors panic and feel overwhelmed.

Morgan Stanley analysts wrote in a report: "Leveraged exchange-traded funds (ETFs) have driven South Korea's KOSPI index and chip stocks to continue to rise, and the market is currently showing trading fatigue." Morgan Stanley maintained the benchmark target of South Korea's KOSPI index at 9,000 points, while lowering the index's 3- to 6-month fluctuation range to 6,000-9,000 points.

In terms of industry allocation, the bank upgraded the communications services sector to an "overweight" rating; it listed the banking sector as its first choice, followed by the information technology, industrial manufacturing and healthcare sectors.

Within the industrial manufacturing sector, defense and military industry is the first choice, followed by shipyards, power generation/nuclear power, and overseas engineering contracting sectors.

Morgan Stanley wrote: "From a medium- to long-term market perspective, the spread of Korean stock market conditions is the general trend; but in the short term, unless reforms bring about revaluation, or the industrial manufacturing sector rises strongly, it will be difficult to significantly reverse the trend of the Korean stock market through market spread alone."

The analysis noted that this could alleviate some of the pressure on policymakers as they consider raising interest rates again. It is reported that the U.S.

Department of Commerce’s Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA) plans to revise its statistical methods for investment management services, computer software and legal services. The updated methodology will be rolled out with the release of August inflation data on September 30 and will be applied retrospectively to the past five years.

That could make inflation readings later this year look slightly lower. The revision is likely to narrow the unusual gap between the personal consumption expenditures price index (PCE) and the consumer price index (CPI). Core CPI inflation was 2.6% in June, while economists estimate core PCE inflation reached 3.3%.

Is TSMC going to generate its own power? New regulations may significantly increase energy costs for wafer fabs in Taiwan TSMC may face a significant increase in its power costs.

Taiwan, China, is reviewing a new energy rule that would allow large commercial electricity users to build their own power generation and energy storage facilities.

It is reported that all commercial entities with an electricity load of more than 5 megawatts are included in the discussion, which means that more than 400 semiconductor, optoelectronics, steel and petrochemical factories and artificial intelligence data centers in Taiwan will be affected. Among them, TSMC may be one of the most affected companies.

It consumes huge amounts of power but is highly dependent on stable and high-quality power supply, because even a millisecond-level voltage sag may cause the entire batch of wafers to be scrapped. TSMC accounts for 9% of Taiwan’s electricity consumption, and S&P estimates that this number will grow to 23.7% by 2030.

If TSMC has to build its own power supply system, the economic benefits of its wafer fabs in Taiwan may be greatly reduced. On the one hand, self-built infrastructure will cost a lot of money, and on the other hand, Taiwan itself relies on energy imports and has no own energy supply channels.

This may accelerate TSMC's capacity expansion in other parts of the world. Currently, TSMC’s total investment commitments in the United States reach US$265 billion, covering 12 planned factories.

Goldman Sachs is making a reasonable structural argument, but I think they are underestimating the cyclical headwinds and overestimating how much central bank buying can support prices in the face of tighter global liquidity.

The bullish thesis rests on the assumption that central bank demand creates a durable floor for gold. That may be true over very long horizons, but central banks are not marginal price setters in the same way that private investors, ETFs, futures markets, and macro funds are. Gold prices are ultimately determined at the margin, and the marginal buyer remains highly sensitive to real interest rates, the US dollar, and global liquidity conditions.

Goldman's own report acknowledges this contradiction. They estimate central banks bought 81 tonnes in May, one of the strongest months on record, yet gold prices have continued to weaken as markets increasingly price a more hawkish Federal Reserve. That tells you something important. Even exceptionally strong official-sector buying has not been sufficient to offset macro headwinds.

The market is simply shifting its focus. Earlier this year, investors were obsessed with fiscal deficits, currency debasement, and aggressive monetary easing. Today the conversation has changed. Oil prices have risen sharply, inflation expectations are climbing again, and the probability of tighter monetary policy has increased. Rising real yields raise the opportunity cost of holding a non-yielding asset like gold, while a stronger US dollar further tightens global financial conditions. Historically, those have been far more powerful drivers of gold than central bank purchases alone.

I also think the diversification narrative is becoming increasingly crowded. Since Russia's reserves were frozen in 2022, reserve diversification into gold has become one of the most widely accepted structural investment themes. When everyone already knows the story, incremental buying becomes progressively less impactful because much of the adjustment has already taken place. Markets do not price today's purchases; they price tomorrow's changes in demand.

Another point that deserves more attention is positioning. Gold ownership among central banks may continue rising, but private investor positioning remains far more influential over shorter investment horizons. If macro funds, systematic strategies, and ETF investors continue reducing exposure because real yields remain elevated, they can easily overwhelm steady official-sector accumulation.

Finally, Goldman assumes the current rate headwinds are temporary because its economists expect no further Fed hikes. That is a significant assumption rather than an established fact. If energy-driven inflation proves more persistent than expected, or if wage pressures remain elevated, policy rates could stay restrictive for much longer. Under that scenario, real yields remain elevated, the dollar stays firm, and gold could face a prolonged valuation reset despite continued central bank buying.

None of this argues that gold is structurally broken. Central bank accumulation is undoubtedly supportive over the long run and likely limits how deep any selloff becomes. However, investors often confuse having a long-term buyer with having an immediate catalyst. Those are two very different things.

My conclusion remains unchanged. Gold is likely to experience a period of multiple compression as investors rotate toward higher-yielding assets in a world of persistent inflation, elevated real interest rates, and a stronger US dollar. Central banks may provide a floor, but they are unlikely to be powerful enough to reverse the direction of the market if private capital continues flowing elsewhere.

MARKET UPDATE — FRIDAY, 17 JULY 2026

The AI trade is facing its most serious test of the year.

Chip stocks are tumbling across Asia, Nasdaq futures are sharply lower and investors are questioning whether enormous spending on AI infrastructure can justify valuations that had already assumed years of exceptional growth.

At the same time, renewed US–Iran fighting is pushing oil higher and reviving inflation fears—just as several Federal Reserve officials begin discussing the possibility of raising interest rates again.

This is not one isolated sell-off.

It is a collision between expensive technology stocks, forced position unwinding, higher energy risk and a more hawkish Fed.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 MARKETS: THE CHIP ROUT DEEPENS

US stock futures point to another lower opening:

🔸 S&P 500 futures: −0.83%

🔸 Nasdaq 100 futures: −1.63%

🔸 Dow futures: −0.58%

🔸 VIX: 18.08, up 8.1%

The Nasdaq is leading the decline because semiconductor, memory and AI-infrastructure shares are once again under heavy pressure.

Nvidia, TSMC and Intel were all lower before the opening bell, while Sandisk, Western Digital, Micron, Applied Materials and other former momentum leaders continued to weaken.

The selling has spread globally.

🔸 Japan’s Nikkei 225 fell approximately 4%

🔸 Taiwan’s benchmark market dropped around 6.5%

🔸 China’s CSI 300 weakened approximately 3.6%

🔸 Europe’s Stoxx 600 fell, with ASML among the biggest technology casualties

South Korea’s market was closed for a holiday, but its semiconductor shares have already experienced severe volatility this month.

Simple explanation

Investors had crowded into the same group of AI and semiconductor companies because their prices kept rising.

Now that those shares are falling, traders using borrowed money, options and leveraged funds may be forced to sell—causing the decline to accelerate.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 SEMICONDUCTORS ARE APPROACHING A BEAR MARKET

The PHLX Semiconductor Index has fallen approximately 19% from its June record high, leaving it close to the 20% decline normally described as a bear market.

Micron has fallen around 30% from its June peak, while several memory and storage companies have lost more than 30% during the past month.

The sector is also heading for its worst week since the tariff-driven market rout of April 2025.

This is happening despite strong results from companies including TSMC and ASML.

TSMC reported a sharp rise in profits, but its shares still fell after investors focussed on plans for another $100 billion of US investment and questioned whether the industry is building too much capacity too quickly.

Simple explanation

Good earnings are no longer enough.

Investors are asking whether companies can earn a satisfactory return on the hundreds of billions being spent on AI chips, data centres and power infrastructure.

When a stock falls despite excellent results, it often means expectations had become even stronger than the actual performance.

Why it matters

The semiconductor sector may still deliver extraordinary long-term growth.

But markets do not value a company only on whether its profits are growing. They also consider how much future growth is already reflected in the share price.

The current sell-off is a reminder that a brilliant business can still be a poor investment when bought at an excessive valuation.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 A MOMENTUM CRASH—NOT YET A BROAD MARKET COLLAPSE

The pressure is most intense among stocks that had risen the fastest.

Goldman Sachs’ high-beta momentum basket has fallen roughly one-third from its peak, while another momentum index has dropped sharply since the technology rally peaked in June.

The decline appears to involve a combination of:

🔸 profit-taking after enormous gains

🔸 forced selling by leveraged investors

🔸 quantitative funds reversing momentum positions

🔸 concern about excessive AI spending

🔸 growing doubts about semiconductor valuations

Yet the broader market has held up better than the headline indexes suggest.

On Thursday, the S&P 500 fell 0.51%, but eight of its 11 sectors rose and the equal-weighted S&P 500 gained nearly 1%.

Financials, healthcare, consumer staples, retailers and transportation companies have recently outperformed many technology stocks.

Simple explanation

The average headline index looks weak because its largest technology companies carry enormous weight.

Underneath the surface, money is not leaving every stock. It is moving from expensive AI winners into other parts of the economy.

Why it matters

This is currently better described as a violent market rotation than a full-scale market breakdown.

However, the risk would increase if heavily leveraged technology investors were forced to sell profitable holdings in other sectors simply to cover losses.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 VOLATILITY IS RISING BENEATH THE SURFACE

The VIX rose above 18, reflecting increasing demand for protection against further market declines.

That remains below the level of 20 commonly associated with heightened fear, but the direction is important.

An even stronger warning comes from the unusually wide gap between the volatility of individual S&P 500 companies and the volatility of the index itself.

Individual shares are moving violently even though the overall index remains close to its record.

Simple explanation

The market index looks relatively calm because gains in some companies are offsetting losses elsewhere.

But underneath that calm surface, individual stocks are swinging sharply.

It resembles a lake that looks still from a distance while powerful currents are moving underneath.

Why it matters

Periods of extremely high disagreement between individual stocks can resolve harmlessly through rotation.

But they can also become dangerous if selling spreads from one crowded sector into the wider market.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 THURSDAY’S CLOSE: TECHNOLOGY HID A STRONGER UNDERLYING MARKET

US markets finished lower on Thursday:

🔸 Dow: −0.20% to 52,552.97

🔸 S&P 500: −0.51% to 7,533.77

🔸 Nasdaq Composite: −1.47% to 25,881.95

The equal-weighted S&P 500 ETF, which gives every company similar importance, rose approximately 1%.

That contrast reveals the real story.

The market was not uniformly weak. A relatively small group of enormous technology companies dragged down indexes even while most sectors advanced.

This broadening could eventually make the bull market healthier—but only if weakness in the AI trade remains contained.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 NETFLIX FALLS AS GROWTH SLOWS

Netflix shares dropped approximately 9% in premarket trading after the company forecast slower third-quarter growth and narrowed its full-year revenue outlook.

Second-quarter revenue rose 13% to $12.6 billion, helped by higher subscription prices and growing advertising revenue.

However, Netflix expects third-quarter revenue growth of approximately 11.7%, which would be its slowest expansion in around three years.

The company also plans to publish less viewing data.

Simple explanation

Netflix is still growing and making strong profits.

The problem is that investors had expected even faster growth.

When a company is valued as a rapid-growth business, merely meeting expectations may not be enough to support its share price.

Why it matters

Netflix’s decline reflects the same challenge affecting semiconductor companies:

Strong companies are being punished because their share prices had left little room for disappointment.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 SPACEX FALLS BELOW ITS IPO PRICE

SpaceX closed Thursday at $131.11, below its reported $135 IPO price for the first time.

The shares weakened further after the 13th Starship test was aborted less than one second before launch when several engines did not start correctly.

The company plans to replace two engines and attempt another launch.

Simple explanation

The rocket’s safety system detected a problem and stopped the launch before liftoff.

That is better than allowing a damaged rocket to launch.

Why it matters

Starship is central to several parts of the SpaceX investment case:

🔸 deploying larger Starlink satellites

🔸 lowering launch costs

🔸 carrying heavier payloads

🔸 lunar and deep-space missions

🔸 potential orbital computing infrastructure

A successful launch would restore confidence.

Continued technical delays would affect nearly every major growth assumption behind the company’s valuation.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 ALPHABET FACES FRESH AI QUESTIONS

Alphabet shares fell more than 4% on Thursday following reports that Google’s Gemini 3.5 Pro model is months behind schedule because of performance problems.

At the same time, rivals in the US and China continue releasing new models.

China’s Moonshot AI introduced its Kimi-K3 model, which reportedly performed strongly in independent model comparisons and contributed to renewed concern that cheaper Chinese systems could challenge expensive US-developed models.

Simple explanation

AI leadership is changing quickly.

A company can spend billions building the best model, only for a competitor to release something stronger or cheaper a few months later.

Why it matters

This uncertainty makes it harder for investors to calculate how long today’s AI infrastructure will remain valuable—and whether the enormous spending behind it will earn acceptable returns.

The technology may transform the economy while still producing disappointing returns for some investors.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 BONDS: YIELDS FALL, BUT THE FED BECOMES MORE HAWKISH

Treasury yields moved lower this morning:

🔸 2-year Treasury: 4.126%, down 1.9 basis points

🔸 10-year Treasury: 4.521%, down 3.6 basis points

🔸 30-year Treasury: 5.055%, down 3.1 basis points

Falling yields would normally suggest investors expect weaker growth or easier monetary policy.

But today’s picture is more complicated.

Several Federal Reserve officials delivered hawkish messages.

🔶 Dallas Fed President Lorie Logan said modestly higher interest rates may be needed because inflation remains too high and current policy may not be sufficiently restrictive.

🔶 Fed Vice-Chair Philip Jefferson indicated that rate increases should be considered if inflation fails to cool, while Kansas City Fed President Jeffrey Schmid also warned that inflation remains excessive.

Simple explanation

The Fed has been waiting for inflation to return sustainably towards 2%.

Recent inflation data improved, but higher oil prices could push petrol, transport and production costs upwards again.

Some officials therefore believe a small rate rise now may prevent a larger inflation problem later.

Why it matters

Markets still expect the Fed to leave rates unchanged at its July meeting.

However, the discussion is shifting from when rates will be cut towards whether another increase may eventually be necessary.

That change matters because higher rates reduce the present value of future profits—placing particular pressure on highly valued technology companies.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 THE US LABOUR MARKET REMAINS RESILIENT

Initial unemployment claims fell by 8,000 to 208,000, significantly below economists’ expectations.

Continuing claims also declined by 16,000 to approximately 1.805 million.

The data support the view that the US remains in a “slow hire, slow fire” labour market.

Companies are hiring cautiously, but widespread layoffs have not developed.

Simple explanation

Relatively few Americans are losing their jobs.

That is good for households and economic growth, but it also gives the Fed less urgency to reduce interest rates.

Why it matters

A resilient labour market strengthens the argument of hawkish Fed officials:

the economy may be capable of tolerating higher interest rates if inflation rises again.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 OIL RISES AS THE US–IRAN WAR WIDENS

Oil remains one of the market’s biggest risks.

Brent crude was trading around $84–$86 per barrel, while WTI was near $79–$81. Both benchmarks were heading for weekly gains of approximately 11%–12% as disruption around the Strait of Hormuz intensified.

The United States carried out a sixth consecutive night of strikes against Iran, targeting coastal surveillance, air-defence, logistics and maritime infrastructure.

Iran responded with missile and drone attacks against US-linked positions across the region and said oil and gas exports through the Strait of Hormuz would remain blocked while US attacks continued.

The conflict also widened geographically.

Iran carried out its first known strike on Syrian territory during the current war, claiming it targeted a US special-operations command centre near al-Tanf. A Syrian military source said the attack missed the base and caused no casualties or material damage.

Iranian attacks were also reported against US-linked facilities in Jordan, Bahrain, Kuwait and Qatar, while US strikes continued around Bandar Abbas, Qeshm Island and southern Iranian transport infrastructure.

Iran has also reportedly asked its Houthi allies to prepare to threaten the Red Sea route if Iranian power infrastructure is attacked.

Simple explanation

The Strait of Hormuz and the Red Sea are two of the world’s most important energy transport routes.

If both become unsafe, fewer oil and gas shipments can reach global markets.

Less supply normally means higher prices.

Why it matters

The wider the conflict spreads, the greater the risk of:

🔸 damage to energy infrastructure

🔸 disruption to shipping and aviation

🔸 attacks on US bases

🔸 involvement by regional allies

🔸 accidental escalation

Higher oil would also affect the wider economy:

🔸 petrol and transport costs rise

🔸 airlines and shipping companies face higher expenses

🔸 manufacturing becomes more costly

🔸 inflation may accelerate

🔸 central banks become less willing to cut rates

🔸 consumer spending may weaken

The biggest market risk is therefore not simply another military strike.

It is a prolonged regional disruption that keeps oil elevated long enough to feed into inflation, company costs and central-bank policy.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 THE MIDDLE EAST’S BORROWING COSTS ARE RISING

Bond investors are demanding their highest additional compensation since 2022 to lend to Middle Eastern governments.

Simple explanation

Investors consider lending to the region more dangerous because the war could affect government finances, oil exports, infrastructure and economic activity.

They therefore demand higher interest payments to accept that risk.

Why it matters

Rising borrowing costs can make it more expensive for governments and companies to refinance debt, fund infrastructure and support their economies.

The financial effects of the conflict are now spreading beyond oil into credit markets.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 IRAQ SEEKS AN ALTERNATIVE TO HORMUZ

Chevron and Iraq are discussing a potential pipeline network that could transport Iraqi oil northwards and then west through Syria to the Mediterranean.

The project could partly revive older pipelines and create export routes through Syria, Turkey or Jordan.

Iraq normally depends heavily on the Strait of Hormuz and has reportedly been forced to reduce production sharply because of the disruption.

Simple explanation

Iraq currently relies heavily on one narrow shipping route.

A pipeline to the Mediterranean would create another exit for its oil.

Why it matters

The proposal reveals a major long-term consequence of the war:

Middle Eastern producers may spend heavily to reduce their dependence on vulnerable maritime chokepoints.

That could benefit pipeline operators, engineering companies and countries positioned along alternative energy corridors.

However, construction, financing and security risks mean the project remains uncertain and would take years to complete.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 UBER MAKES A $14.8 BILLION DELIVERY BET

Uber has agreed to acquire Delivery Hero in a transaction valuing the German company’s equity at $14.8 billion, subject to regulatory and shareholder approval.

The combination would create the largest food-delivery group outside China and significantly expand Uber’s international reach.

Simple explanation

Uber is buying one of its biggest competitors instead of continuing to fight it market by market.

A larger network may lower costs and allow Uber to sell rides, food delivery and subscriptions to the same customers.

Why it matters

The deal shows that scale has become increasingly important in delivery businesses, where thin margins make it difficult for smaller companies to compete.

It may also face intense regulatory scrutiny because the combined company would hold powerful positions in numerous markets.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 ELI LILLY MAKES A MAJOR BET ON PSYCHEDELIC MEDICINE

Eli Lilly agreed to acquire AtaiBeckley for an initial $2.8 billion, with another $1 billion potentially payable if development milestones are achieved.

AtaiBeckley is developing psychedelic-based treatments for mental-health conditions.

Simple explanation

Lilly is paying a premium for experimental medicines that may offer new ways to treat illnesses such as depression.

The treatments remain risky because they still need to prove they are safe, effective and commercially viable.

Why it matters

The acquisition is an important vote of confidence from one of the world’s largest pharmaceutical companies.

It suggests psychedelic medicine is moving from a speculative niche towards mainstream pharmaceutical investment.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 CHINA CHALLENGES THE US OVER GLOBAL AI LEADERSHIP

Twenty-nine countries have signed an agreement establishing the World Artificial Intelligence Cooperation Organization, based in Shanghai.

Chinese President Xi Jinping presented China as a supporter of more open and internationally shared AI development, particularly for emerging economies.

Simple explanation

The competition over AI is no longer only about building the strongest model.

It is also about deciding the international rules, standards and infrastructure that other countries will use.

Why it matters

The world may gradually develop competing AI ecosystems:

🔸 a US-led system focussed on controlled supply chains and security

🔸 a China-led system emphasising access, sovereignty and development

Countries may eventually face pressure to choose between technologies, standards and suppliers from rival blocs.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 DEEPSEEK’S IMPLIED VALUE REACHES $52 BILLION

A Chinese regulatory filing implied that DeepSeek was valued at approximately $52 billion during a private transaction.

The figure is not an official valuation announced by the company, but it provides the clearest public evidence yet of how investors are valuing the Chinese AI developer.

Why it matters

DeepSeek’s valuation shows that investors believe lower-cost Chinese AI models could become serious global competitors.

It also reinforces the central challenge facing the AI market:

the cost of developing frontier models is rising rapidly, while the useful life of any individual technological advantage may be becoming shorter.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 CRYPTO: ETF DEMAND REMAINS MIXED

US spot cryptocurrency ETFs recorded mixed flows on 16 July:

🔸 Bitcoin: approximately $79.2 million of net inflows

🔸 Ethereum: approximately $28 million of net outflows

🔸 XRP: approximately $6.8 million of inflows

🔸 Solana: approximately $1.7 million of inflows

Simple explanation

More money entered Bitcoin, XRP and Solana investment funds than left them, while Ethereum funds experienced withdrawals.

Why it matters

Bitcoin continues to attract institutional demand, but crypto remains vulnerable to the same forces affecting other risk assets:

🔸 higher interest-rate expectations

🔸 rising oil and inflation risk

🔸 a stronger dollar

🔸 declining appetite for speculative investments

ETF inflows may provide support, but they do not make crypto immune to a broader market deleveraging event.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 WHAT IT MEANS

Today’s market is sending eight important messages.

1️⃣ The AI trade is being repriced—not disproved

The long-term importance of artificial intelligence has not disappeared.

But investors are questioning whether every AI-linked company deserves the extreme valuation created during the rally.

2️⃣ Excellent earnings may already be reflected in prices

TSMC and ASML delivered strong results, yet their shares weakened.

That is a warning that expectations—not merely profits—determine short-term market reactions.

3️⃣ Leverage can turn an ordinary correction into a violent sell-off

Options, leveraged funds and momentum strategies amplify gains on the way up.

They can amplify losses when the direction reverses.

4️⃣ Market breadth remains the key dividing line

Financials, healthcare, consumer staples, retailers and transports are holding up better.

As long as capital rotates rather than disappears, the broader bull market may remain intact.

5️⃣ Oil is the bridge between war and monetary policy

The conflict matters to investors because disrupted energy supplies can raise inflation.

Higher inflation can keep interest rates elevated or force the Fed to tighten again.

6️⃣ The Fed is no longer discussing only rate cuts

A growing group of policymakers believes rates may not be restrictive enough.

This creates a more difficult environment for expensive, long-duration growth assets.

7️⃣ The global AI race is becoming geopolitical

Chinese models, capital and governance institutions are challenging American dominance.

AI is becoming both an investment theme and a struggle over international influence.

8️⃣ Selectivity matters more than direction

This is no longer a market where buying every AI-related company produces easy gains.

Investors must distinguish between:

🔸 strong technology and strong economics

🔸 revenue growth and profitable growth

🔸 an exceptional company and an attractive valuation

🔸 a temporary correction and a broken investment thesis

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

MARKET OUTLOOK

The immediate market risk remains tilted lower while semiconductor selling continues.

The most important signals to watch are:

🔸 whether the PHLX Semiconductor Index crosses the 20% bear-market threshold

🔸 whether selling spreads from chips into financials, healthcare and defensives

🔸 whether the VIX rises decisively above 20

🔸 whether oil holds above the mid-$80s or moves towards $90

🔸 whether the US 10-year Treasury yield remains near 4.5% despite hawkish Fed remarks

For now, this remains a severe technology and momentum correction inside a broader market that is still functioning.

But the combination of leverage, expensive valuations, war-driven oil inflation and hawkish central-bank rhetoric means investors should not treat the sell-off casually.

SUPPLY & DEMAND — THE FUNDAMENTAL PARADOX:

Before the war, the oil market was heading toward a massive surplus:
• IEA forecast: 2-4 million barrels per day oversupply
• Non-OPEC production (US, Brazil, Guyana) surging
• OPEC+ preparing to unwind cuts
• Goldman Sachs base case: $52 WTI

The war didn't change demand — it ANNIHILATED supply.

The Strait of Hormuz normally moves 17-20 million barrels per day. Bypass capacity maxes out at ~8 mb/d. The remaining 9+ mb/d gap cannot be rerouted. Global supply collapsed by 3.7-3.9 mb/d. Demand only fell by ~1 mb/d.

Net result: a 3-4 mb/d DEFICIT — despite weak demand.

Tech fell 4.3% and the SOX entered a 20% correction. Intuitive Surgical fell 9% on a beat. Gold dropped 2.4% to $4,015 while crypto rose, the cleanest evidence yet that the store-of-value flows are rotating back. Next week: Alphabet, Tesla, Intel, Texas Instruments, IBM, ServiceNow, 3M, American Express, Verizon.

Sentiment: The internals and the index are telling opposite stories, and this time the internals are the bullish one. Softening advance-decline lines had weighed on sentiment coming in, but this week the average stock rose while the cap-weighted index fell, the mirror image of the narrow melt-up that defined May. The VIX held steady through six days of airstrikes. Crypto sentiment continues thawing: three green weeks, ETH leading, Fundstrat pounding the table, and the gold-to-crypto rotation resuming. The nervous signals: record margin debt is now leveraged against a chip sector down 20%, the CLARITY Act stalled, and oil’s 20% two-week surge is quietly rebuilding the exact inflation pressure the market just celebrated escaping.

Last week I said the whole summer was riding on the second decimal place of Tuesday’s CPI. The number came in better than anyone dared model, the biggest monthly decline since the pandemic, and it should have launched the S&P through 7,620. Instead the index fell 1.55%, because the same six days produced the biggest oil spike in six years and a chip correction that turned the market’s engine into its anchor. That is the trade now: the disinflation dividend from June is real but already spent, the July CPI will carry $81 oil inside it, and the AI complex that holds up 40% of the index is down 20% from its highs and reporting earnings next week. The healthy part of this market has never been healthier: record bank profits, 8 of 11 sectors rising, breadth improving, crypto basing with ETH leading exactly like it did before the last bottom. The fragile part has never been more concentrated: one sector, one narrative, one week of mega-cap earnings to defend a correction already in progress. Alphabet and Tesla report while bombs fall and the Strait stays shut. If mega-cap tech delivers, the rotation was a gift and the record is back within reach. If it stumbles, there is no CPI print good enough to catch the knife. The banks just told you what volatility is worth. Buckle up for the week that prices it.

Saronic Picks Brownsville for $3 Billion ‘Port Alpha’ Autonomous Shipyard

Mike Schuler  July 16, 2026

Autonomous vessel developer Saronic has selected Brownsville, Texas, as the site of a planned $3 billion shipyard that the company says will dramatically expand U.S. capacity to build autonomous vessels while supporting broader efforts to revive the nation’s shipbuilding industry.

The facility, dubbed Port Alpha, is expected to begin construction later this year and open in 2028. Located on an initial 835-acre site at the Port of Brownsville with room to expand to nearly 4,400 acres, the shipyard is designed to produce large autonomous vessels while incorporating advanced manufacturing, robotics and software-driven production systems.

The latest announcement marks another major milestone for the Austin, Texas-based company, which has rapidly emerged as one of the leading players in the maritime autonomy sector. It also represents the next phase of Saronic’s manufacturing expansion. After acquiring Louisiana-based Gulf Craft last year and committing $250 million to modernize the shipyard for production of its Marauder autonomous vessel, the company is now moving to build an entirely new facility aimed at scaling autonomous shipbuilding in the United States.

“America’s maritime future depends on our ability to build again,” Saronic Co-Founder and CEO Dino Mavrookas said in announcing the project. “Port Alpha is our commitment to that mission. Built from the ground up to deliver ships at a speed and scale not seen since World War II, this investment is about more than constructing a shipyard. It is about rebuilding the industrial capacity, workforce, and manufacturing advantage required to ensure American maritime leadership for decades to come.”

Saronic said it selected Brownsville following a year-long search that evaluated locations across the East, West and Gulf coasts. The company cited the region’s available workforce, deepwater access, transportation infrastructure and room for future expansion.

According to the company, Port Alpha will be capable of building vessels up to 850 feet long initially, with future expansion supporting vessels exceeding 1,200 feet. The project is expected to create up to 10,000 direct jobs over the next decade, ranging from skilled trades such as welding and machining to robotics, software engineering and naval architecture. Saronic also said it plans to partner with Texas educational institutions to develop workforce training and apprenticeship programs.

The announcement comes just days after Saronic’s Corsair autonomous surface vessel made its combat debut during U.S. military operations against Iranian naval infrastructure near the Strait of Hormuz, underscoring how rapidly autonomous maritime systems are moving from experimentation into operational service.

U.S. Central Command confirmed earlier this week that three Saronic-built Corsair unmanned surface vessels struck a submarine and ship maintenance facility at Iran’s Bandar Abbas Naval Base, marking what the military described as the first combat use of American sea drones. The operation followed another milestone in June, when a Navy-operated Corsair rescued two U.S. Army aviators after their Apache helicopter crashed off the coast of Oman.

The announcement aligns with the Trump administration’s push to rebuild domestic shipbuilding capacity through initiatives including the Maritime Action Plan and the proposed SHIPS for America Act, which seek to reverse decades of decline in U.S. commercial shipbuilding and reduce reliance on foreign yards.

For Saronic, the Brownsville project marks a significant step beyond building autonomous vessels toward creating the industrial capacity needed to manufacture them at scale. Together with its Louisiana facility, the company says the investments represent a multi-billion-dollar commitment to expanding U.S. shipbuilding capacity and accelerating production of autonomous maritime systems.

Separately, UK Maritime Trade Operations has now reported one unidentified vessel on fire approximately eight nautical miles northwest of Kumzar, Oman.

UKMTO says the cause of that fire remains unverified.

❓ Still unknown:

• Whether the UKMTO vessel is one of the tankers described by Iran
• Whether two vessels actually exploded
• Vessel names, flags and cargoes
• Whether mines, missiles, drones or another cause were involved
• Crew casualties or injuries
• Environmental damage
• What role, if any, American forces played

🎯 Gunny’s Take:

Do not automatically merge these reports.

The IRGC claims two tankers exploded on an unsafe route. UKMTO has confirmed only that one unidentified vessel is on fire near Kumzar—and has not determined why.

They could be connected, but that connection has not been established.

What is confirmed is that the Strait is becoming more dangerous by the hour while Iran and the United States enforce competing restrictions on commercial shipping.

📊 60-Second Market Lesson: EIA Crude Oil Inventories

What is it?
A weekly report from the U.S. Energy Information Administration showing the change in commercial crude oil stockpiles held in the U.S.

Why does it matter?
It's a direct read on U.S. oil supply-demand balance. A build = supply outpacing demand (bearish bias). A draw = demand outpacing supply (bullish bias).

How it moves markets:
🛢️ WTI & Brent crude — most direct impact
💵 USD — indirectly, via energy-linked inflation expectations
📈 Energy equities (XLE, oil majors)
⚠️ Can trigger sharp intraday volatility, especially on a big deviation from consensus

📅 Release: July 22, 2026
🔮 Forecast: +1.978M bbl
📉 Previous: -1.692M bbl

A swing from a large draw to an expected build — worth watching how price reacts if the actual print surprises either direction.

Der Irankrieg ist in eine neue Phase eingetreten und die Märkte preisen erst die Hälfte davon ein.

Iran hat die Straße von Hormus erneut für geschlossen erklärt, bis auf Weiteres. Der Schiffsverkehr liegt bei praktisch null.

Beide Seiten greifen jetzt zivile Infrastruktur an. Kuwait meldet den zweiten Treffer auf seine Kraft- und Entsalzungsanlage innerhalb von 48 Stunden, dazu brennende Treibstofftanks am Flughafen. 90% des kuwaitischen Trinkwassers kommen aus Entsalzung. Die USA treffen Brücken, Energieanlagen und den Hafen Chabahar.

Kuwait kürzt vorsorglich die eigene Ölproduktion.

Der eigentliche Schock läuft aber über Gas: Katar hat als erster Golfstaat sämtliche Schifffahrt gestoppt. Ras Laffan, das größte LNG-Exportzentrum der Welt, liefert nicht mehr.

Europa trifft das mitten in der Einspeichersaison. TTF notiert über 56 Euro/MWh, plus 29% allein im Juli. Jede Woche ohne Katar-LNG macht die Speicherfüllung vor dem Winter teurer.

Der Ölmarkt zeigt die Knappheit längst in den Produkten: Diesel-Crack-Spread auf Rekordhoch bei 66 Dollar pro Barrel. Brent schloss am Freitag bei 88,10 Dollar, plus 4,6%.

Und die nächste Eskalationsstufe ist bereits definiert: Iran hat die Houthis angewiesen, sich bereitzuhalten, das Rote Meer zu schließen, falls Washington die iranische Strominfrastruktur angreift. Das Weiße Haus prüft parallel den Einsatz von Bodentruppen.

Zwei Chokepoints gleichzeitig, Öl und Gas gleichzeitig, mitten in der Einspeichersaison. Das ist das Szenario, das gerade auf dem Tisch liegt.

⚠️Watch this week: Monday oil price action, EIA on Wednesday (SPR + Cushing), and whether diesel tightness starts feeding back into crude more visibly.

With the current Middle East escalation (2 US servicemen KIA in Jordan this weekend, US already hitting back) and Russia’s diesel export ban still in force until 31 July after Ukraine’s deep refinery strikes, the most likely path is continued SPR draws, Cushing staying pinned near the operational floor, and diesel crack spreads widening further.

No immediate off-ramp in sight on either front. Baseline remains thin usable buffers + two live supply disruptions through at least Q3.
